citations.desc: Consignments to carriers and intermediate handlers.
citations.narrative:
On December 29, 2025, the registrant accepted a dog for transport more than 6 hours before the scheduled departure
time of a flight.
Per the registrant's digital records, one adult Corgi was accepted for transport at 7:20 PM on December on 29, 2025,
approximately 13.5 hours before the scheduled departure. The animal was scheduled to fly from Chicago ORD to Seoul
ICN Airport via Taipei, Taiwan (Waybill #695-5496736). The flight departed approximately on time at 8:59 AM the
following morning on December 30, 2025. The records are congruent with employees' stated recollection of the animal's
transport.
Accepting an animal more than 6 hours before its scheduled departure time extends their time confined in transport
enclosures, which can increase increase risk of adverse health and welfare effects of animal transport.
Carriers must not accept a dog or cat for transport in commerce more than 4 hours before the scheduled departure time
of the primary conveyance on which the animal is to be transported. However, a carrier or intermediate handler may
agree with anyone consigning a dog or cat to extend this time by up to 2 hours.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
3.14(a) 
   
Consignments to carriers and intermediate handlers. 
On December 29, 2025, the registrant accepted a dog for transport more than 6 hours before the scheduled departure 
time of a flight.  
 
Per the registrant's digital records, one adult Corgi was accepted for transport at 7:20 PM on December on 29, 2025, 
approximately 13.5 hours before the scheduled departure. The animal was scheduled to fly from Chicago ORD to Seoul 
ICN Airport via Taipei, Taiwan (Waybill #695-5496736). The flight departed approximately on time at 8:59 AM the 
following morning on December 30, 2025. The records are congruent with employees' stated recollection of the animal's 
transport.  
 
Accepting an animal more than 6 hours before its scheduled departure time extends their time confined in transport 
enclosures, which can increase increase risk of adverse health and welfare effects of animal transport. 
 
Carriers must not accept a dog or cat for transport in commerce more than 4 hours before the scheduled departure time 
of the primary conveyance on which the animal is to be transported. However, a carrier or intermediate handler may 
agree with anyone consigning a dog or cat to extend this time by up to 2 hours. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
